title: Setting up a small quarantine pen
subtitle: Where new stock land before they join the flock, and where sick stock go when they need to leave it.
excerpt: A working smallholding wants a small pen kept aside for two jobs: holding new sheep, goats, pigs, or poultry away from the resident stock for the first three weeks, and isolating any home animal that needs treatment. A walk through siting, hurdles, biosecurity, and the small list of kit that lives in the pen permanently.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Codes of Practice for sheep, goats, and pigs (statutory UK welfare codes). AHDB Beef & Lamb biosecurity factsheet. SAC Consulting Scotland, on-farm quarantine notes. NBU BeeBase guidance on the equivalent quarantine for new hives.

glossaryTerms.definition: The set of routine measures that stop disease, parasites, and weed seeds moving from one part of the holding (or one farm) to another. Hand-washing, dedicated boots for the quarantine pen, and feed kept separate are the working basics.
Sheep scab; a mite infestation that spreads fast and is notifiable in Scotland. The single most-checked-for problem during a new-sheep quarantine in the UK.
A wormer dose given to incoming sheep on arrival, using a wormer class the holding has not used recently, to clear any resistant worms the new stock might carry in.
A short freestanding panel of welded steel or hardwood, used to build temporary pens and races. Standard UK lengths are 4 ft, 6 ft, and 8 ft. Two clip-pins join one hurdle to the next.

recipeTools.notes: Only if the corner posts are going in for a permanent pen rather than a hurdle build.

content.text: Every smallholding that buys in stock benefits from a quarantine pen. New sheep arriving from a market or a farm sale carry whatever the seller's flock was carrying. New chickens carry whatever the breeder's coop had. New pigs carry whatever the supplier did not bother to mention. A three-week separation before the new stock meet the resident stock gives the keeper time to look, treat, and refuse to mix if something turns up.

The same pen doubles as a hospital pen during the year. A ewe needing a course of antibiotics, a hen on the mend from impacted crop, a pig recovering from a foot injury all want the same thing: a small space away from competition, with food, water, and the keeper visiting twice a day. Setting the pen up once and keeping it ready saves the panicked half-hour at the start of every incident.
Where to put it
Three things to weigh. Distance from the main flock matters most for
biosecurity

: ideally fifty metres or more from the field gate the resident sheep use, with no shared fence line. Walkable from the house, so a 6 am check does not feel like a hike. Sheltered from the prevailing wind, with hard standing or well-drained ground underneath. The corner of a barn, a paddock against a stone wall, or a small fenced patch behind the holding all work. Avoid the field gate, the muck heap, and anywhere the rest of the flock can stand at the fence and breathe over.
Pen size by species

attrs.title: Minimum quarantine pen sizes
attrs.body: Two sheep or goats: 3 by 3 m (9 m squared). Two weaner pigs: 3 by 3 m plus a small arc for shelter. Six adult laying hens: 2 by 2 m run plus a small coop or covered shelter. Two adult rabbits: 1.5 by 1.5 m run with a hutch. These are minimums for a three-week quarantine; longer holds need more space.

content.text: What to build it from
For occasional use, four
hurdles

tied at the corners with orange baler twine give a pen that goes up in twenty minutes and packs away in five. For regular use, a permanent timber-post-and-stock-wire pen is worth the day's work to put in: corner posts, a single gate, stock wire with two strands of barbed wire above for sheep, an electric line for pigs. Either way, leave a wide single gate (not double) and a small slip-pen entrance the keeper steps through without opening the main gate. The slip-pen entrance is where dedicated quarantine boots and a foot-dip live.
The fixed kit in the pen
A hay rack at the back wall.
A galvanised water trough or a clipped-on water bucket on the opposite side.
A small shelter (a pig arc, a coop, a low timber lean-to) sized to species.
A bedding store: a tarpaulin-covered straw bale at one corner is enough.
A muck fork hanging on the gate post, used only in the pen and stored there.
A foot-dip tray at the entrance with a strong veterinary disinfectant, refreshed weekly when the pen is in use.
Biosecurity routine when stock is in the pen

Visit the quarantine pen last on the daily round, never first. Keep a pair of wellies and an old waterproof in a sealed tub at the entrance and change into them at the slip-pen step. Wash hands at the house tap before and after each visit. Feed and bedding for the quarantine pen come from a separate sub-bag, not the main store. Mucking-out goes into a dedicated heap at least twenty metres from the main muck heap, and that heap waits six months before any use.
The three-week protocol for new sheep
On arrival day: pen, water, hay, and a hands-off twelve hours so the stock settles. Day one: a
quarantine drench
using a wormer class the holding has not used in the last twelve months. Day three to five: a close look-over for
scab

, lameness, condition, fleece state, and eye colour against the gum (the FAMACHA card check). Day seven to ten: a foot trim and a footbath if anything looked off in the first inspection. Day fourteen: a second worm-egg-count sample to the vet. Day twenty-one: if everything is clean, the new stock joins the main flock at the next quiet moment. If anything is unclear, hold longer and re-test.
The shorter protocol for hospital use

When the pen is in use for a home animal under treatment, the biosecurity routine is the same but the timeline is set by the condition rather than a fixed three weeks. Most home cases (impacted crop, foot rot, mastitis under treatment) clear inside a week. The animal returns to the flock once the cause is resolved and she has stood her own for three steady days.
Cleaning between uses

Strip the bedding out and fork it onto the dedicated heap. Scrape the floor of the shelter, wash down with a veterinary detergent, and let dry. Wash the feeders, the trough, and the muck fork. Refresh the foot dip. Leave the pen empty and open to the air for a fortnight before the next use if possible; the daylight, the drying, and the rest do as much for the next animal as the disinfectant.
What you might have found

attrs.heading: Quarantine pen problems
attrs.intro: What can go wrong during a quarantine hold and how to recover.

items.symptom: Pen built right against the resident-flock field fence.
items.cause: The site chosen was too convenient.
items.fix: Move the pen. A shared fence line means nose-to-nose contact and the quarantine is functionally useless. Even a 3 metre gap with a closed alley between makes a real difference.

items.symptom: Resident dog has been crossing the quarantine pen daily.
items.cause: Dog routes were not re-planned when the pen was set up.
items.fix: Confine the dog away from the pen for the hold. Dogs carry mud, dung, and parasites between pens as efficiently as any boot.

items.symptom: New ewe scoured on day three of the hold.
items.cause: Diet change, transport stress, or a wormer kicking in.
items.fix: Hold the position; do not move her on. Take a dung sample to the vet for a faecal egg count and an oocyst check (for coccidia). Adjust diet if dung is still loose after 48 hours.

items.symptom: Pen is sodden underfoot after a wet fortnight.
items.cause: The site drains badly and the bedding cannot keep up.
items.fix: Lift the stock onto fresh hard standing (a clean barn corner) while the original pen dries and gets a stone or hardcore base. A long-term wet pen is a foot rot factory.

items.symptom: Bird in the quarantine pen has died and the cause is not obvious.
items.cause: Could be many things; could be something contagious.
items.fix: Bag the carcass, store cool, contact the vet for a post-mortem. Do not bring the rest of the stock into the main flock until the cause is known.

content.text: When the pen is empty

Most of the year, the quarantine pen sits empty. Keep it that way: do not store hay, the trailer, or seasonal kit in it. An empty pen ready to use the same evening is what makes the difference when a new pair of pigs arrives unannounced or a hen comes out of the coop limping. The half-hour of monthly tidying is what keeps the pen useful.
